takeaways: Senior housing occupancy is set to top 90% in 2026 — likely the highest in 20 years of NIC MAP data — while new construction has fallen to 2012 lows, tightening supply just as the oldest boomers turn 80.
CRE capital is reopening: the MBA forecasts a 24% jump in lending volume, Fannie and Freddie each get $88B multifamily caps (+20%), and the 10-Year Treasury has steadied near 4.0–4.25%.
Proptech investment surged to ~$1.7B in January 2026 (+176% YoY) as agentic AI moves from experiment to operational tool across underwriting, leasing, and fraud prevention.

items.headline: Senior housing occupancy on track to exceed 90% in 2026
items.summary: The average senior housing occupancy rate is expected to surpass 90% in 2026, potentially the highest in the 20 years NIC MAP has tracked the data. Demand from older adults keeps rising even as development pipelines stay historically thin — a favorable demand backdrop for Lloyd Jones communities.

items.headline: New senior living construction falls to lowest level since 2012
items.summary: Units under construction dropped to the lowest level since 2012, with the industry adding just 0.4% of inventory in Q1 2026. Tariffs, construction labor shortages, and cost pressures are tempering a hoped-for development uptick — constraining future supply and supporting pricing power for existing operators.

items.headline: CRE lending volume forecast to jump 24% in 2026
items.summary: The Mortgage Bankers Association projects a 24% increase in commercial real estate lending this year, with bank, insurance, and private-credit originations all rising at double-digit rates. Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac each get $88B multifamily caps, up 20% — expanding financing options for senior housing acquisitions and refis.

items.headline: Proptech funding hits $1.7B in a single month, up 176%
items.summary: Investors poured roughly $1.7 billion into proptech in January 2026, a 176% jump from January 2025. AI-centered proptech grew at a 42% annualized rate in 2025 — nearly double non-AI peers — signaling fast-maturing tools for underwriting, rent rolls, and operations relevant to senior housing operators.

items.headline: Oldest baby boomers turn 80, driving record senior housing demand
items.summary: The oldest baby boomers turn 80 in 2026, pushing senior housing demand toward record levels. With pipelines thin and occupancy climbing, the demographic wave underpins a multi-year demand story for operators and investors like Lloyd Jones.

items.headline: Senior living firm lines up $300M–$600M acquisition pipeline
items.summary: Senior Living Transformation Company (SLTC) signaled 'hockey-stick' growth ahead, with a pipeline of likely deals totaling $300 million to $600 million. The expansion underscores renewed capital appetite and consolidation momentum in the senior living sector.

items.headline: Texas senior housing transactions hit record $2.9B
items.summary: Texas senior housing recorded $2.9 billion in transactions in 2025, an all-time high in rolling four-quarter volume, with Houston seeing a sharp rise in luxury senior living demand. The Sun Belt momentum is directly relevant to Lloyd Jones's regional footprint.

items.headline: CRE sales volume seen rising 15–20% as capital re-enters
items.summary: Colliers forecasts a 15% to 20% increase in CRE sales volume in 2026 as institutional and cross-border capital returns. Capital is flowing again with rates easing and leasing fundamentals stabilizing — a more constructive deal environment for senior housing buyers and sellers.

items.headline: 10-Year Treasury steadies near 4.0–4.25%, anchoring loan pricing
items.summary: As of mid-May 2026, the 10-Year Treasury has held in a 4.00%–4.25% range since mid-2025, with commercial mortgage rates roughly 5.23%–8.92%. A more stable benchmark gives borrowers clearer underwriting assumptions for new senior housing financing.

items.headline: Agentic AI moves from experiment to real estate workhorse
items.summary: Agentic AI is emerging as proptech's next phase, automating complex workflows and shifting from content generation to autonomous decisions in procurement, pricing, and operations. Operators are beginning to deploy these tools to cut costs and improve decision-making across portfolios.

items.headline: AI-driven rental and mortgage fraud spurs proptech defenses
items.summary: More than 12,000 real-estate fraud complaints were filed with the FBI in 2025, totaling over $275 million in reported losses, with AI fueling the surge. Proptech firms are racing to deploy detection tools — a rising operational risk and screening priority for housing operators.

items.headline: CEOs double down on AI, planning to lift spend to 1.7% of revenue
items.summary: CEO ownership of AI strategy has doubled year over year, and companies plan to roughly double AI spending in 2026 — from about 0.8% to 1.7% of revenues. Leaders are increasingly accountable for turning AI from pilots into measurable value.

items.headline: Flattening hierarchies reshape management — with burnout risk
items.summary: Economic pressure and AI-driven efficiency are flattening org structures, with most companies expected to have fewer layers by 2030. But thinning middle management can break internal communication and fuel burnout, a caution for operators streamlining teams.

items.headline: 89% of employees say wellbeing lifts their performance
items.summary: New Work-Life-Wellness 2026 data shows 89% of employees perform better when prioritizing wellbeing, framing wellness as one of the highest-ROI talent strategies. For care-sector employers, wellness investment ties directly to engagement, retention, and resident-facing performance.

items.headline: Texas senior housing demand reflects broader Sun Belt strength
items.summary: Luxury senior housing in metros like Houston hit record highs as boomers age in place regionally. The trend reinforces the Sun Belt as a priority growth corridor for senior living investment and development.

items.headline: Affordability for middle-income seniors stays unresolved
items.summary: Affordability for middle-income seniors remains one of the industry's most pressing challenges, and nonprofits and cities are stepping up amid 'tremendous need' for affordable options. The gap represents both a social challenge and a potential product opportunity for operators.

items.headline: VCs chase vertical AI for underwriting and inspections
items.summary: In 2026, venture capital is targeting vertical-specific AI models for underwriting, rent rolls, inspections, insurance risk, and financial reconciliation. These purpose-built tools promise efficiency gains across the real estate operating stack.

items.headline: Leadership development is HR's top priority into 2026
items.summary: Leadership development remains the No. 1 HR priority heading into 2026, and nearly 80% of leaders say the CHRO role has grown dramatically in importance. Building leadership pipelines proactively is becoming a core resilience strategy.

items.headline: 'Emotional fitness' emerges as a new wellness category
items.summary: 2026 introduces emotional fitness as a distinct health category, emphasizing cognitive performance and emotional resilience, powered by AI-driven analytics and at-home biomarker tests. Personalized, preventive wellness is moving mainstream.

items.headline: Wellness 'third places' redefine workplace connection
items.summary: Wellness-focused 'third places' — gyms, studios, and wellness hubs outside home and office — are becoming the new epicenter of workplace relationships. Employers are increasingly expected to enable holistic health across fitness, mindfulness, nutrition, and sleep.
